---
title: "13 · Similarity and Scale"
subtitle: "Same shape, different size — and how lengths, areas, and volumes scale"
lesson-id: "t2.similarity-scale"
tier: 2
prerequisites: ["t1.ratio-proportion", "t1.shapes-angles"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 25
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T1 L09 — Ratio and Proportion](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)
   and [T1 L12 — Shapes and Angles](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)?
2. Recall: doubling a square's side **quadruples** its area? (T1 L13
   Stretch S1 — the square-cube law.)
3. Comfortable with the idea that *photographs and maps* are scaled
   versions of the real thing?
:::

## Touch

Pick a **scale factor** $k$. The widget shows two triangles: the
original (orange), and a *similar* triangle scaled by $k$ (blue).

```{ojs}
viewof scaleK = Inputs.range([0.5, 3], {value: 1.5, step: 0.1, label: "Scale factor k:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 720, H = 380, pad = 30;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px; background:#fafafa;");

  // Original triangle
  const o = {x: 80, y: 220};
  const tri = (origin, k, fill, stroke, label) => {
    const p1 = {x: origin.x, y: origin.y};
    const p2 = {x: origin.x + 60 * k, y: origin.y};
    const p3 = {x: origin.x, y: origin.y - 80 * k};
    svg.append("polygon").attr("points", `${p1.x},${p1.y} ${p2.x},${p2.y} ${p3.x},${p3.y}`)
      .attr("fill", fill).attr("stroke", stroke).attr("stroke-width", 2).attr("opacity", 0.7);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", origin.x).attr("y", origin.y + 25).attr("text-anchor", "start")
      .attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", stroke).attr("font-weight", "bold").text(label);
    // Side lengths
    svg.append("text").attr("x", origin.x - 12).attr("y", origin.y - 40 * k)
      .attr("text-anchor", "end").attr("font-size", "11").attr("fill", stroke).text(`${(8 * k).toFixed(1)}`);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", origin.x + 30 * k).attr("y", origin.y + 14)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "11").attr("fill", stroke).text(`${(6 * k).toFixed(1)}`);
    // Hypotenuse
    const hyp = Math.sqrt((6 * k) ** 2 + (8 * k) ** 2);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", origin.x + 35 * k).attr("y", origin.y - 35 * k)
      .attr("font-size", "11").attr("fill", stroke).text(`${hyp.toFixed(1)}`);
    return hyp;
  };

  tri(o, 1, "#ffe0b2", "#e65100", "Original");
  tri({x: 380, y: 290}, scaleK, "#bbdefb", "#0d47a1", `Scaled (k = ${scaleK.toFixed(1)})`);

  // Right side: ratios
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 600).attr("y", 50).attr("font-size", "14").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("fill", "#1f2933").text("Ratios:");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 600).attr("y", 80).attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#1f2933").attr("font-family", "monospace")
    .text(`Sides: ×${scaleK.toFixed(1)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 600).attr("y", 105).attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#1f2933").attr("font-family", "monospace")
    .text(`Area:  ×${(scaleK ** 2).toFixed(2)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 600).attr("y", 130).attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#1f2933").attr("font-family", "monospace")
    .text(`(area = k² for shapes)`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", 600).attr("y", 165).attr("font-size", "11").attr("fill", "#666")
    .text(`(angles unchanged: same shape)`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

When the scale factor is $k$, every length multiplies by $k$. The
*shape* is the same (same angles); only the *size* changes. That's
**similarity**.

## Wonder

1. Set $k = 2$. The new triangle has all sides twice as long. By how
   much does its **area** multiply?
2. The two triangles look the same but at different sizes. What
   *property* survives scaling? What changes?
3. Could a square and a rectangle ever be similar?

## Discover

### Problem 1

A photograph is **8 cm wide and 6 cm tall**. To enlarge it to 12 cm
wide while keeping its shape, what should the new height be?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Scale factor: $k = 12/8 = 1.5$. Apply to height too.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$k = 1.5$. New height = $6 \times 1.5 = 9$ cm.

The width:height ratio stays $4:3$. Scaling preserves *aspect ratio*
— same shape, different size.
</details>

### Problem 2

Two triangles have angles $(40°, 60°, 80°)$. Are they similar?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Same angles → same shape → similar (by definition).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes, similar.** Two triangles are similar **iff** they have the same
angles. (A condition called **AAA** — angle, angle, angle. Just AA is
enough since angles sum to 180°.)

This is one of the most useful facts in geometry. If you can show two
triangles share two angles, *all corresponding sides are proportional*
— and you can solve for unknown lengths.
</details>

### Problem 3

A 1.8 m tall person casts a 2.4 m shadow. At the same time, a tree
casts an 8 m shadow. How tall is the tree?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The triangles formed by sun-person-shadow and sun-tree-shadow are
similar (same sun angle).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{\text{height}}{\text{shadow}} = \dfrac{1.8}{2.4} = \dfrac{T}{8}$.
Cross-multiply: $T = \dfrac{1.8 \times 8}{2.4} = \dfrac{14.4}{2.4} = 6$
m.

**Tree is 6 m tall.** This is the classic **shadow problem**, used by
the ancient Greeks (Thales) to estimate the height of pyramids — the
*first* recorded use of similarity for measurement.
</details>

### Problem 4

A model car is **1:18 scale** (every length is 1/18 of the real car).
The real car is **4.5 m long** and **2 m wide**. What are the
dimensions of the model? What is the **ratio of areas** (real : model)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Lengths scale by 1/18. What about areas?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Model length: $4.5/18 = 0.25$ m = 25 cm. Model width: $2/18 \approx
0.111$ m = 11.1 cm.

**Ratio of areas:** $(18)^2 = 324$. The real car's *surface area* is
324 times the model's. The model's area is 1/324 of the real area.
</details>

### Problem 5

If the model car (Problem 4) is solid — same density as the real car —
how does its **volume** compare to the real car's? What about its mass?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume scales by $k^3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Real volume = $(18)^3 = 5832$ × model volume. The real car has 5832 ×
the volume.

Mass is proportional to volume (for same material and density), so the
real car has 5832 × the mass of the solid model. If the model weighs
100 g, the real car would weigh 583 kg — close to a real small car.

**The square-cube law again** (T1 L13 Stretch S1): scaling lengths by
$k$ scales areas by $k^2$ and volumes by $k^3$. This is *the* reason
elephant legs are proportionally thicker than mouse legs, why scaling
buildings up requires new structural ideas, and why scaling a recipe
2× doesn't always work for a cake (heat penetrates by surface area but
must reach interior volume).
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
Around **240 BCE**, the Greek scholar **Eratosthenes** measured the
*circumference of the Earth* using nothing but similar triangles, a
stick, and a pair of cities. At noon on the summer solstice, the sun
shone straight down a deep well in Syene (modern Aswan) — but in
Alexandria, 800 km north, a vertical stick cast a small shadow at the
*same* moment. The angle of that shadow (about $7.2°$, or one-fiftieth
of a circle) had to equal the angle subtended at Earth's centre between
the two cities. So Earth's circumference was $50 \times 800 \approx
40{,}000$ km — within a few percent of the modern value.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**Shape is preserved by scaling — but size, area, and volume are
not.** Doubling a model doubles its lengths, *quadruples* its
surfaces, and *octuples* its insides. That mismatch is why a
scaled-up ant collapses, a scaled-up bridge needs reinforcement, and a
shadow can measure a planet.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

Two figures are **similar** if one can be obtained from the other by
**scaling** — multiplying every length by the same constant $k$
(possibly with rotation or reflection).

Equivalently, two figures are similar iff:

- All corresponding angles are equal.
- All corresponding sides are in the same ratio $k$.

For two **triangles**, similarity is determined by *angles alone*:

- **AA (Angle-Angle):** if two angles match, the third must (since
  angles sum to 180°), so the triangles are similar.
- **SAS:** two sides in ratio with the angle between them equal.
- **SSS:** all three sides in ratio.

**Scaling laws (the square-cube law):**

| Quantity | Scales by |
|---|---|
| Lengths (sides, perimeter) | $k$ |
| Areas (surface, cross-section) | $k^2$ |
| Volumes (mass, capacity) | $k^3$ |

:::

> *Same angles, sides in ratio — that is similarity;*
> *Lengths × k; areas × k²; volumes × k³.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A miniature toy is **1:50 scale** of a real bus. The real bus is
> **12 m long** and weighs **15 tonnes**. What is the length of the
> toy? If the toy is solid (same density as the real bus), how much
> would it weigh?

A competent solver applies the scaling laws:

1. **Length:** $12 \div 50 = 0.24$ m = 24 cm.
2. **Mass** (proportional to volume): scales by $(1/50)^3 = 1/125000$.
   Toy mass $= 15{,}000{,}000 \div 125{,}000 = 120$ g.

**Toy is 24 cm long and (if solid) about 120 g.**

(Real toys are usually *hollow plastic*, much lighter than this — a
deliberate departure from solid scaling.)

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Two similar triangles. The smaller has sides 3, 4, 5. The
larger is scaled by $k = 4$. What are its sides?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$12, 16, 20$.
</details>

**W2.** Scale factor 3. By how much does area multiply? Volume?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Area $\times 9$, volume $\times 27$.
</details>

**W3.** A scale model is 1:200. A 50 m flagpole has model height of...?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$50 / 200 = 0.25$ m = 25 cm.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Two photos are similar: small photo $4 \times 6$, large photo
$10 \times 15$. Confirm they are similar (same aspect ratio). What is
the scale factor of areas?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4:6 = 2:3$ and $10:15 = 2:3$. Same aspect ratio — similar.
Length scale: $10/4 = 2.5$. Area scale: $(2.5)^2 = 6.25$.

Check: small area $= 24$, large area $= 150$. $150/24 = 6.25$ ✓.
</details>

**P2.** A pizza place sells **two pizza sizes**: 10-inch (Rs. 200) and
20-inch (Rs. 600). Which gives more pizza per rupee?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pizza area scales like the *square* of the diameter.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Area scale (20-inch vs 10-inch): $(20/10)^2 = 4$. The big pizza has
**4 times the area** of the small one.

Cost-per-area: small = Rs. 200/area; big = Rs. 600/(4·area) = Rs.
150/area. **The big pizza gives 33% more pizza per rupee.**

This square-scaling is why doubling pizza diameter way more than
doubles the food. Many fast-food chains exploit this — "small" pizzas
are deliberately less efficient per rupee.
</details>

**P3.** A small triangle has sides 5, 12, 13 (a Pythagorean triple).
A similar triangle has its longest side equal to 39. Find its other
two sides.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Scale factor: $39/13 = 3$. Other sides: $5 \times 3 = 15$ and $12
\times 3 = 36$. So 15, 36, 39 — itself a Pythagorean triple ($15^2 +
36^2 = 225 + 1296 = 1521 = 39^2$ ✓).
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Why a giant ant cannot exist.** An ordinary ant carries about
50× its own weight. If you scaled an ant up to the size of a person
(say, 1000× longer), what happens to its strength-to-weight ratio?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Strength scales like cross-sectional muscle area ($k^2$). Weight
scales like volume ($k^3$). The ratio strength/weight scales like
$k^2 / k^3 = 1/k$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Strength: scales by $k^2 = 10^6$.
Weight: scales by $k^3 = 10^9$.
Strength-to-weight ratio: scales by $1/k = 1/1000$.

**The giant ant would be 1000× weaker per unit weight** than its
small cousin. Where the small ant carries 50× its weight, the giant
ant could carry only $50/1000 = 0.05$ of its own weight — i.e., it
couldn't even support itself.

The square-cube law sets *physical limits* on biology: this is why
big land animals (elephants) have proportionally massive legs, why
whales must live in water (where buoyancy supports their weight),
and why insects cannot be scaled up. Galileo wrote about exactly this
in 1638 — and modern biology still finds the principle indispensable.
</details>

**S2.** A computer simulation models a virtual world at **1:1000
scale**. To realistically simulate gravity, what should the simulation
do with the time scale (frame rate, physics step) so that "scaled
seconds" feel right?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Falling time under gravity scales with $\sqrt{\text{height}}$. So if
heights scale by $1/1000$, times scale by $\sqrt{1/1000} = 1/\sqrt{1000}
\approx 1/31.6$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For physical realism: time should scale by $\sqrt{1/1000} \approx
1/31.6$. So the simulation should run **31.6× slower** than wall-clock
time to look right. (Or, equivalently, gravity should be reduced.)

This is why miniature movie sets (1980s practical effects era — *Star
Wars*, *Lord of the Rings*) had to be filmed at very high frame rates,
then played back normally — the *physics* of falling at miniature scale
looks unnatural at normal speed. Modern CGI handles this correctly by
explicit physical simulation in the right units.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Similar means identical."** No — similar shapes have the same
   *shape* (angles) but possibly different *size*. Identical = similar
   *and* same scale.
2. **"Doubling dimensions doubles everything."** No — doubles
   lengths, *quadruples* areas, *octuples* volumes. The square-cube
   law catches everyone.
3. **"AAA is the most general similarity test."** For triangles, AAA
   (or AA) suffices. For *quadrilaterals*, AAA is *not* enough — you
   need additional information about side lengths. (Two parallelograms
   with the same angles can have different shapes.)
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Two figures are [similar](/glossary.qmd#similar-figures) when one is
   a scaled copy of the other** — same angles, sides in the same
   ratio.
2. **For triangles, two equal angles are enough.** That single
   shortcut (AA) lets you measure shadows, model trains, pyramids, and
   distant stars.
3. **The [square-cube law](/glossary.qmd#square-cube-law) governs all
   scaling.** Lengths scale by $k$, areas by $k^2$, volumes by $k^3$
   — and biology, engineering, and cooking all bend to it.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain *why* doubling all sides of a square quadruples
   its area, using the rectangle picture?
2. Without scrolling back, can you find the scale factor between
   triangles with sides 3,4,5 and 12,16,20?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $k = 4$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[14 · Quadratic Equations](../14-quadratic-equations-intro/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (giant ant) — the strength-to-weight scaling
  is one of the most consequential ideas in physics and biology.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P4 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T1 L09 — Ratio and Proportion](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)**
- **[T1 L12 — Shapes and Angles](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 3 — Trigonometry** — trigonometric ratios depend only on the
  angles of a right triangle, by similarity.
- **Tier 4 — Calculus & physics** — scaling arguments are everywhere
  in dimensional analysis, fluid dynamics, biomechanics.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development & graphics.** Sprite scaling, level-of-detail
techniques (smaller models when far away), perspective rendering —
all rest on similarity. The graphics card scales mesh data on the fly
using the same proportionality you used for the toy car.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Scaling arguments and *dimensional
analysis* are some of the most powerful tools in applied mathematics
and theoretical physics — including in fluid dynamics, finance
(option pricing scales with $\sqrt{T}$), and statistical mechanics.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Biology & medicine.** Allometry — the study of how biological
quantities scale with body size — uses these ideas constantly.
Drug doses scale with body mass, but metabolic rate scales with
mass to the 3/4 power (Kleiber's law) — surprising and
important.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Maps and blueprints, model trains, scaled
prototypes (engineering, architecture), photography, recipe
adjustment, taxes (proportional vs progressive scaling).
:::
